description: The introduction of the National Minimum Wage in the United Kingdom was a landmark Labour Party manifesto pledge that took effect on April 1, 1999. It faced intense opposition from business groups like the Confederation of British Industry, who feared job losses, while being championed by unions and the Low Pay Commission.

key_events.description: Labour Party wins the 1997 General Election with a manifesto commitment to a minimum wage.
The Low Pay Commission publishes its first report recommending an initial rate of £3.60 per hour.
Business groups launch a public campaign warning that the wage floor will lead to mass unemployment.
The National Minimum Wage Act 1998 fully takes effect in April 1999, proving early apocalyptic economic predictions false.

calibration_notes: This scenario represents a classic 'policy implementation vs. industry fear' dynamic, useful for calibrating how public support shifts from theoretical consensus to apprehension during the implementation phase.
